findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, enhancing soft tissue mass situated within the lateral retropharyngeal space, specifically in the expected location of the node of Rouvière. The mass often demonstrates central low attenuation, indicative of necrosis, which is highly suspicious for a malignant process.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include an identifiable primary mucosal lesion, most commonly a squamous cell carcinoma arising from the nasopharynx or oropharynx. Additional cervical lymphadenopathy may also be present, alongside obliteration of the adjacent parapharyngeal fat planes without overt inflammatory stranding.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of extensive prevertebral soft tissue swelling, profound retropharyngeal fat stranding, or an enhancing thick-walled fluid collection with locules of gas. Furthermore, there is no contiguous extension of an inflammatory process into the mediastinum.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an isolated, centrally necrotic mass in the lateral retropharyngeal space without adjacent profound inflammatory changes.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Nodal metastases (SCC involving the lateral retropharyngeal node of Rouvière), given the characteristic anatomical location and the presence of central necrosis. Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck is the most frequent cause of retropharyngeal nodal metastasis, frequently originating from a clinically occult primary in the nasopharynx.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Infection/abscess, however this typically presents with a rim-enhancing fluid collection surrounded by extensive inflammatory fat stranding, often extending from infected tonsillar tissue. Furthermore, a retropharyngeal abscess usually correlates with acute clinical symptoms such as fever, severe sore throat, and an elevated white cell count, which would not typically align with a painless necrotic node.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Nodal metastases, given that if this were actually a benign abscess, erroneously treating it as metastatic malignancy would lead to an inappropriate surgical neck dissection and risk catastrophic spread of infection to the mediastinum. However, since metastatic SCC remains my primary consideration, the main neoplastic alternative I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, which can present similarly but typically lacks central necrosis prior to treatment.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify the site of the occult primary tumor, particularly evaluating the nasopharynx, oropharynx, and oral cavity.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the chronicity of the lymphadenopathy and to see if a small mucosal primary was evident on previous head and neck imaging.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend an MRI of the neck and face, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex soft tissue anatomy of the pharyngeal mucosal space, searching for the primary mucosal lesion, and assessing perineural spread.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to evaluate the primary mucosal surfaces and assess the margins of the necrotic nodes, alongside T2-weighted sequences and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to detect restricted diffusion typical of highly cellular tumors like SCC.
management.intervention: If a primary tumor is not immediately obvious, I would specify an ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration or core biopsy of a more accessible cervical node, or panendoscopy with targeted mucosal biopsies by the ENT surgeons, to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the size and location of the retropharyngeal node, the presence of extracapsular spread, and the status of any potential primary mucosal sites to guide definitive chemoradiotherapy or surgical neck dissection.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a well-circumscribed, enhancing soft tissue mass situated within the lateral retropharyngeal space, specifically in the expected location of the node of Rouvière. The mass often demonstrates central low attenuation, indicative of necrosis, which is highly suspicious for a malignant process. Supporting features include an identifiable primary mucosal lesion, most commonly a squamous cell carcinoma arising from the nasopharynx or oropharynx. Additional cervical lymphadenopathy may also be present, alongside obliteration of the adjacent parapharyngeal fat planes without overt inflammatory stranding. Importantly, there is no evidence of extensive prevertebral soft tissue swelling, profound retropharyngeal fat stranding, or an enhancing thick-walled fluid collection with locules of gas. Furthermore, there is no contiguous extension of an inflammatory process into the mediastinum. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an isolated, centrally necrotic mass in the lateral retropharyngeal space without adjacent profound inflammatory changes.

The appearances are most in keeping with Nodal metastases (SCC involving the lateral retropharyngeal node of Rouvière), given the characteristic anatomical location and the presence of central necrosis. Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck is the most frequent cause of retropharyngeal nodal metastasis, frequently originating from a clinically occult primary in the nasopharynx. The principle differential would be Infection/abscess, however this typically presents with a rim-enhancing fluid collection surrounded by extensive inflammatory fat stranding, often extending from infected tonsillar tissue. Furthermore, a retropharyngeal abscess usually correlates with acute clinical symptoms such as fever, severe sore throat, and an elevated white cell count, which would not typically align with a painless necrotic node. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Nodal metastases, given that if this were actually a benign abscess, erroneously treating it as metastatic malignancy would lead to an inappropriate surgical neck dissection and risk catastrophic spread of infection to the mediastinum. However, since metastatic SCC remains my primary consideration, the main neoplastic alternative I would want to exclude is Lymphoma, which can present similarly but typically lacks central necrosis prior to treatment.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify the site of the occult primary tumor, particularly evaluating the nasopharynx, oropharynx, and oral cavity. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the chronicity of the lymphadenopathy and to see if a small mucosal primary was evident on previous head and neck imaging. I would recommend an MRI of the neck and face, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex soft tissue anatomy of the pharyngeal mucosal space, searching for the primary mucosal lesion, and assessing perineural spread. I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to evaluate the primary mucosal surfaces and assess the margins of the necrotic nodes, alongside T2-weighted sequences and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) to detect restricted diffusion typical of highly cellular tumors like SCC. If a primary tumor is not immediately obvious, I would specify an ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration or core biopsy of a more accessible cervical node, or panendoscopy with targeted mucosal biopsies by the ENT surgeons, to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis. This should go to the Head and Neck Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the size and location of the retropharyngeal node, the presence of extracapsular spread, and the status of any potential primary mucosal sites to guide definitive chemoradiotherapy or surgical neck dissection.
